claims.text: The Flash Update on flash floods and landslides in Chattogram Division has been prepared by the Needs Assessment and Information Management Working Group and published on 09 July 2026, reporting 7 districts affected, 536,000 people affected, 11 deaths, 17,000 people temporarily displaced and 1,700 shelters activated.

Flash Update #2 (dated 11 July 2026, published 13 July) reports that 10 districts have been affected, more than 1.07 million people have been impacted, over 36,000 people remain temporarily displaced, and 26 casualties have been reported.

Situation Report #1 (dated 13 July 2026, published 14 July) reports 51 reported fatalities and more than 1.11 million people affected across 10 districts (Chattogram, Cox's Bazar, Bandarban, Khagrachhari, Rangamati, Moulvibazar, Sunamganj, Habiganj, Sylhet and Cumilla), with more than 38,000 people staying in over 1,000 evacuation shelters as of 12 July, and significant damage to homes, water systems, roads, bridges and public infrastructure.

Oxfam situation report on the Bangladesh flash floods (15 July 2026): 1.11 million people affected across 10 districts, 53 fatalities and 39 injuries reported as of 15 July; Chattogram district was hardest-hit, with Chattogram city recording 412.3mm of rain in 24 hours, the highest single-day July rainfall in 42 years.

BDRCS/IFRC nationwide situation report (14 July 2026): 1,074,534 people affected (NAWG figure) across 10 districts, about 38,500 people temporarily displaced (MoDMR figure) across 1,000+ shelters, 51 deaths, 267,918 households stranded, including 15 Rohingya refugee deaths reported by UNHCR among the Cox's Bazar camp toll.

published.description: Days of torrential monsoon rain beginning around 4 July 2026 triggered flash floods and landslides across ten districts of southeastern Bangladesh, centred on Cox's Bazar and Chattogram. Chattogram city recorded its highest single-day July rainfall in 42 years. The disaster killed dozens of people, including Rohingya refugees in the Cox's Bazar camps, and stranded more than a million residents, prompting the UN Resident Coordinator's office and the government's Disaster Management and Relief Ministry to scale up an emergency response.

impact.summary: As of an Oxfam situation report (15 July 2026, via ReliefWeb), monsoon-triggered flash floods and landslides had affected 1.11 million people across 10 districts of southeastern Bangladesh, with 53 fatalities and 39 injuries reported; Chattogram district was hardest-hit, recording 412.3mm of rain in 24 hours, its highest single-day July rainfall in 42 years. A corroborating BDRCS/IFRC nationwide situation report (14 July 2026) put the toll at 51 deaths (including 15 Rohingya refugees in Cox's Bazar camps, per UNHCR), 1,074,534 people affected, about 38,500 people temporarily displaced across 1,000+ shelters, and 267,918 households stranded. Flood and landslide risk continues, with the threat shifting from southeastern districts toward the north and northeast (Sylhet, Sunamganj, Rangpur).

location.region: Chattogram, Cox's Bazar, Bandarban, Rangamati, Khagrachhari, Moulvibazar, Sunamganj, Habiganj, Sylhet and Cumilla districts, southeastern Bangladesh

published.name: Monsoon Floods & Landslides — Chattogram Division, Bangladesh

claims.text: GDACS's flood event report (GLIDE FL-2026-000105-CHN, covering 06 June 2026 to 07 July 2026) assessed an overall Orange alert, stating that 'flood can have a medium humanitarian impact based on the magnitude, exposed population and vulnerability,' and records official figures of 12 deaths and 85,197 people displaced across affected areas of China.

Re-checked 9 July 2026: GDACS's event report for the flood still shows the reporting window closed, spanning 06 Jun 2026 01:00 UTC to 07 Jul 2026 01:00 UTC, with the same 12 deaths and 85,197 displaced figures unchanged since the window closed, confirming no further escalation has been recorded.

published.description: Torrential rain from the remnants of Typhoon Maysak triggered severe flooding across Guangxi, Hunan, Hubei and neighbouring provinces of southern and central China in early July 2026, pushing rivers and reservoirs past danger thresholds and submerging villages. GDACS assessed an overall Orange alert for medium humanitarian impact, citing 12 deaths and 85,197 people displaced. GDACS's reporting window for the event closed on 7 July with no further escalation observed.

impact.summary: GDACS's event report (covering 6 June to 7 July 2026) assessed an overall Orange alert for medium humanitarian impact from flooding across southern and central China, citing 12 deaths and 85,197 people displaced. The flooding intensified in early July as the remnants of Typhoon Maysak brought torrential rain to Guangxi, Hunan and Hubei. As of 9 July 2026, GDACS's reporting window remains closed at 7 July with no updated death or displacement figures, indicating the event has concluded.

location.region: Guangxi, Hunan and Hubei provinces, with further effects reported in Guizhou, Liaoning, Guangdong, Jiangxi and Taiwan

published.name: Southern China Floods (Guangxi, Hunan, Hubei)

claims.text: On 17 May 2026 the WHO Director-General determined that the epidemic of Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus in the DRC and Uganda constitutes a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC).

In total, 1481 confirmed cases; 1460 from the Democratic Republic of the Congo, 20 from Uganda and one from France (linked to DRC); and 454 deaths including two from Uganda, have been reported. As of 1 July 2026, the Democratic Republic of the Congo had reported 1,460 confirmed cases including 452 deaths (case fatality ratio 30.9%) and 213 recovered; as of 2 July 2026 Uganda had reported 20 confirmed cases including two deaths and one probable case (deceased), with 16 recovered.
As of 4 July, the country's [DRC] Government has recorded 1,561 confirmed cases, including 506 deaths and 254 people recovered. More than 10,000 contacts are being monitored.

As of 9 July 2026, the Democratic Republic of the Congo reported a total of 1,792 confirmed cases, including 625 related deaths (from data up until 8 July), an increase of 33 new confirmed cases and 25 related deaths since the prior update, with cases concentrated in Ituri province (1,631 cases, 535 deaths) and North Kivu (158 cases, 89 deaths). Uganda has reported a total of 20 confirmed cases, including two deaths, with the last confirmed case reported on 21 June and no new cases since.

On 12 July 2026, the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) reported a total of 1,926 confirmed cases, including 702 related deaths (from data up until 11 July). A total of 753 patients are hospitalised in isolation. Ituri province remains the most affected, with 1,745 cases, including 601 deaths, reported from 26 of 36 health zones. In North Kivu, 73 cases, including 97 deaths, have been reported from 11 of 34 health zones. In South Kivu, three cases, including one death, have been reported from one of 34 health zones. In Haut-Uele, one case has been reported and in Tshopo four cases. As of 12 July 2026, in Uganda a total of 20 confirmed cases, including two deaths, have been reported by the Ministry of Health; the last confirmed case was reported on 21 June with no new cases since. On 10 July 2026, the US CDC reported that a US citizen working for a humanitarian organisation in DRC had tested positive for Bundibugyo virus; the patient was medically evacuated to Germany on 13 July 2026, in addition to an imported case reported in France on 24 June 2026.

On 13 July 2026, the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) reported a total of 1,963 confirmed cases, including 719 related deaths (data up until 12 July), an increase of 31 new confirmed cases and 10 deaths since the previous update; 736 patients are hospitalised in isolation and 333 have recovered. Ituri province remains the most affected, with 1,772 cases including 608 deaths, reported from 26 of 36 health zones; North Kivu has reported 177 cases including 100 deaths; South Kivu 3 cases including 1 death; Haut-Uele 7 cases including 7 deaths; and Tshopo 4 cases including 3 deaths. Uganda has reported 20 confirmed cases including two deaths, with the last confirmed case on 21 June and no new cases since.

On 14 July 2026, the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) reported a total of 2,011 confirmed cases, including 754 related deaths (data up until 13 July), an increase of 54 new confirmed cases and 28 deaths since the previous update; 753 patients are hospitalised in isolation and 366 have recovered. Ituri province remains the most affected, with 1,808 cases including 631 deaths, reported from 26 of 36 health zones; North Kivu has reported 182 cases including 106 deaths; South Kivu 3 cases including 1 death; Haut-Uele 14 cases including 13 deaths; and Tshopo 4 cases including 3 deaths — 45 of 140 health zones affected in total. Uganda has reported 20 confirmed cases including two deaths, with the last confirmed case on 21 June and no new cases since; 67.4% of identified case contacts in affected provinces are under follow-up.

WHO Director-General's opening remarks at the media briefing of 16 July 2026: "So far, 2073 cases have been reported, with 796 deaths" in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. "In Uganda, the last confirmed case is being discharged from care today, starting the 42-day countdown" toward declaring the country's outbreak over, with 20 cases and two deaths.

As of 14 July 2026 (data up until 14 July), the Democratic Republic of the Congo reported 2,073 confirmed cases including 796 related deaths. Ituri province remains the most affected with 1,863 cases including 668 deaths, reported from 26 of 36 health zones; North Kivu has reported 189 cases including 111 deaths; South Kivu 3 cases including 1 death; Haut-Uele 14 cases including 13 deaths; and Tshopo 4 cases including 3 deaths. Uganda has reported 20 confirmed cases including two deaths; its last confirmed case, reported on 21 June, was discharged from care on 16 July 2026 after two negative tests, starting the 42-day countdown toward declaring Uganda's outbreak over.

published.description: The Democratic Republic of the Congo declared its 17th Ebola outbreak — caused by Bundibugyo virus — on 15 May 2026, centred in Ituri Province. On 17 May 2026 the WHO Director-General declared the DRC and Uganda epidemic a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC). Confirmed cases and deaths have continued to climb into July, with imported cases confirmed in France and, most recently, a US citizen medically evacuated to Germany. Uganda's last confirmed patient was discharged on 16 July, starting the 42-day countdown toward declaring that country's outbreak over.

impact.summary: As of 14 July 2026 data reported by the WHO Director-General on 16 July 2026, the Democratic Republic of the Congo had reported 2,073 confirmed cases including 796 deaths, concentrated in Ituri province (1,863 cases, 668 deaths) and North Kivu (189 cases, 111 deaths), with further cases in South Kivu, Haut-Uele and Tshopo. Uganda's confirmed case count remains at 20, including two deaths; its last confirmed patient was discharged from care on 16 July 2026 after two negative tests, starting the 42-day countdown toward declaring that country's outbreak over. WHO says the outbreak continues to outpace the response, with more than 80% of new infections occurring outside known contact-tracing lists.

location.region: Ituri Province (Mongbwalu), DRC, with cases in Uganda and imported cases in France and Germany

published.name: Ebola (Bundibugyo virus) — DR Congo & Uganda

claims.text: In the space of just three weeks, the UN Human Rights Office documented 15 drone strikes on El Obeid, which killed at least 45 civilians. El Obeid has more than half a million residents, together with at least 100,000 internally displaced persons, and the number of newly displaced people across Kordofan has risen by nearly two-thirds in only three months.

G7 Foreign Ministers' joint statement on El Obeid, Sudan (14 July 2026): condemns ongoing RSF drone strikes on El Obeid and obstruction of humanitarian access, references the precedent of atrocities in El Fasher, and calls for an immediate ceasefire and compliance with the Jeddah Declaration to protect civilians.

NRC report (posted 7 July 2026) on El Obeid: families are dying from drone strikes and from hunger as the siege continues, with an aid convoy struck the preceding week; essential services and food access are collapsing further as the blockade persists.

published.description: Rapid Support Forces (RSF) paramilitary forces have besieged El Obeid, the capital of North Kordofan state, subjecting the city of more than half a million residents to relentless drone strikes and blockade conditions. The United Nations Human Rights Office documented 15 drone strikes on the city and surrounding areas in three weeks, and UN officials have warned of an imminent risk of mass atrocities similar to those already documented in El Fasher, Darfur. G7 foreign ministers condemned the siege on 14 July 2026, and the UN Human Rights Council launched a Fact-Finding Mission inquiry into the situation.

impact.summary: The UN Human Rights Office documented 15 drone strikes on El Obeid and surrounding areas over three weeks that killed at least 45 civilians. El Obeid has more than half a million residents, together with at least 100,000 internally displaced persons sheltering in the city. As of the G7 foreign ministers' joint statement of 14 July 2026, the RSF siege and near-daily drone strikes were continuing, with routes into the city still blocked except toward the east; no allowlisted source has published an updated aggregate casualty count since the initial 3 July UN report. UN officials continue to warn of the risk of large-scale atrocities.

event.scale: City under siege; UN human-rights atrocity-risk warning

published.name: Siege of El Obeid — North Kordofan, Sudan

claims.text: WHO/Europe's statement of 30 June 2026 reports that Spain's mortality monitoring system has already estimated more than 300 heat-associated excess deaths in just a few days, that emergency medical calls in France have risen by up to 50% in some cities, that London's ambulance service recorded its highest-ever number of life-threatening emergency calls in a single day, and that Italy reported 5 deaths in 24 hours during the heatwave.

WHO/Europe's statement of 7 July 2026, titled 'Extreme heat: more deadly weeks may still lie ahead for the European Region', warns that the current heatwave risk to health remains active and that heat-health action plans save lives.

WHO/Europe's statement of 16 July 2026, "Planning for a warmer world": "This summer's toll is still being counted but, already, data from just 5 countries shows nearly 10,000 excess deaths due to heat." WHO Regional Director Dr Hans Henri P. Kluge: "In the past 4 years alone, heat has claimed more than 200,000 lives, while heat-related mortality has risen by 30% over the past 20 years."

published.description: A record-breaking series of heatwaves has gripped Western and Southern Europe since around 21 June 2026, straining health systems and emergency services across France, Spain, Belgium, the Netherlands, Italy and the UK. WHO/Europe issued successive public statements describing rising heat-associated mortality; its 16 July 2026 statement reported that data from just five countries already showed nearly 10,000 excess deaths from heat this summer, describing the season as a preview of a warmer future.

impact.summary: WHO/Europe's 30 June 2026 statement reported more than 300 heat-associated excess deaths in Spain within a few days, alongside a 50% surge in emergency medical calls in some French cities, the highest single-day volume of life-threatening ambulance calls ever recorded in London, and 5 deaths in 24 hours reported in Italy. Its follow-up statement of 16 July 2026, "Planning for a warmer world," reported that data from just five countries already showed nearly 10,000 excess deaths due to heat this summer, and that heat has claimed more than 200,000 lives across the WHO European Region in the past four years, with heat-related mortality up 30% over the past two decades. No source has declared the heatwave over.

location.country: France (heatwave affects multiple Western European countries)
location.region: Western and Southern Europe: France, Spain, Belgium, Netherlands, Italy, United Kingdom

event.scale: Continental heatwave / WHO public health emergency statement

published.name: 2026 European Heatwave — Public Health Emergency

claims.text: GDACS's forest fire event report for France (event 1029331, covering 04-07 July 2026) assessed an Orange alert, stating 'forest fire can have a medium humanitarian impact based on the burned area and the affected population and their vulnerability,' with 6,561 hectares burned and 5,570 people exposed in the burned area.

Re-checked 9 July 2026: GDACS's event report now shows a start date to last-detection window of 04 Jul 2026 to 08 Jul 2026 (duration 4 days), with 6,576 hectares burned and 5,570 people affected in the burned area; no thermal anomaly has been detected since 08 Jul 2026, indicating the fire is no longer actively spreading.

published.description: A wildfire broke out in the Pyrénées-Orientales department of southern France in early July 2026, burning through mountainous terrain in the foothills of the Pyrenees near Perpignan. The EU's Copernicus Emergency Management Service activated rapid mapping for the area, and GDACS assessed an Orange alert for medium humanitarian impact as the fire spread. No further thermal anomalies have been detected since 8 July, indicating the fire is now contained.

impact.summary: GDACS's event report assessed an Orange alert (medium humanitarian impact) for a wildfire burning in the Pyrénées-Orientales department of France between 4 and 8 July 2026, citing about 6,576 hectares burned and roughly 5,570 people exposed within the fire-affected area. As of 9 July 2026, GDACS records no thermal anomaly detections since 8 July, indicating the fire is now contained.

published.name: Wildfire — Pyrénées-Orientales, France

claims.text: A magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck at 5.5996, 125.0647 (offshore Sarangani, Philippines) on 7 June 2026 at 23:37 UTC, at 35 km depth, generating a maximum tsunami wave of 1.93 metres; GDACS issued an orange alert for medium humanitarian impact with about 2.9 million people exposed to Modified Mercalli Intensity VII or greater.

At least 19 people were killed, 12 remain missing and at least 134 were injured after the 7.8 magnitude earthquake struck Sarangani province on 8 June 2026, according to OCHA, with authorities noting the figures were provisional as assessments continued; more than 3.2 million learners were affected with classes suspended in over 6,200 schools.

By 23 June, government figures reported 77 deaths, with about 1.6 million people affected across Regions IX, XI, XII, and BARMM. PHIVOLCS recorded more than 9,400 aftershocks and further seismic activity expected. More than 80,000 houses have been damaged, including nearly 15,000 destroyed, with 884 damaged infrastructure losses and 130 affected roads and 47 bridges limiting access to isolated communities. Eighteen local government units (LGUs) have declared a state of calamity.

DSWD DROMIC Report #58, as of 09 July 2026, 6AM, reports 391,891 families (1,655,313 persons) affected in 650 barangays across Regions IX, XI, XII and BARMM, with 24,097 families (92,256 persons) still displaced — 7,008 families (28,090 persons) inside 74 evacuation centers and 17,089 families (64,166 persons) staying outside evacuation centers with relatives or friends. A total of 121,426 houses were damaged, of which 19,305 were totally destroyed and 102,121 partially damaged.

DSWD DROMIC Report #60, as of 10 July 2026, 6AM, reports 391,871 families (1,655,313 persons) affected in 650 barangays across Regions IX, XI, XII and BARMM, with 24,919 families (95,129 persons) still displaced — 7,830 families (30,963 persons) inside 79 evacuation centers and 17,089 families (64,166 persons) staying outside evacuation centers with relatives or friends. A total of 121,307 houses were damaged, of which 19,188 were totally destroyed and 102,119 partially damaged.

DSWD DROMIC Report #64, as of 13 July 2026, 6PM, reports a total of 391,891 families (1,655,313 persons) affected in 650 barangays in Regions IX, XI, XII and BARMM. There are 25,048 families (95,629 persons) still displaced in Regions XI, XII and BARMM — 7,959 families (31,463 persons) inside 80 evacuation centers in Regions XI and XII, and 17,089 families (64,166 persons) temporarily staying with relatives or friends. A total of 121,426 houses were damaged, of which 19,305 were totally damaged and 102,121 were partially damaged. A total of ₱1,156,615,547.45 worth of assistance has been provided to affected families, of which ₱1,156,595,547.45 came from DSWD and ₱20,000.00 from LGUs.

DSWD DROMIC Report #66, as of 16 July 2026, 6PM, reports a total of 391,975 families (1,655,686 persons) affected in 651 barangays across Regions IX, XI, XII and BARMM. There are 24,472 families (93,971 persons) still displaced — 7,383 families (29,805 persons) inside 78 evacuation centers and 17,089 families (64,166 persons) staying outside evacuation centers with relatives or friends. A total of 121,362 houses were damaged, of which 19,193 were totally destroyed and 102,169 partially damaged.

published.description: A magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck offshore Sarangani province in the southern Philippines on 8 June 2026 (7:37am local time / 7 June 23:37 UTC), triggering a tsunami of nearly 2 metres and widespread damage across Mindanao. GDACS issued an orange alert for medium humanitarian impact. The confirmed death toll and number of affected people climbed through late June as damage assessments continued, and eighteen local government units have declared a state of calamity. Displacement and housing-damage figures have continued to be tracked into mid-July as recovery continues.

impact.summary: As of DSWD DROMIC Report #66 (16 July 2026, 6PM), 391,975 families (1,655,686 persons) are affected across 651 barangays in Regions IX, XI, XII and BARMM, with 24,472 families (93,971 persons) still displaced — 7,383 families (29,805 persons) inside 78 evacuation centers and 17,089 families (64,166 persons) staying outside evacuation centers with relatives or friends. A total of 121,362 houses have been damaged, including 19,193 totally destroyed and 102,169 partially damaged. Government figures still report 77 deaths, 1,339 injured and 31 missing (OCHA, as of 23 June — the latest allowlisted-source casualty figure; no OCHA narrative update has superseded it despite newer DROMIC reports). GDACS assessed an orange alert (medium humanitarian impact) with about 2.9 million people exposed to severe shaking and a tsunami of up to 1.93 metres; eighteen local government units have declared a state of calamity.

location.region: Offshore Sarangani province, Mindanao (near General Santos)

published.name: Mindanao M7.8 Earthquake & Tsunami — Philippines

claims.text: GDACS's forest fire event report for Portugal (event 1029260, covering 02-08 July 2026) assessed an Orange alert, stating 'forest fire can have a medium humanitarian impact based on the burned area and the affected population and their vulnerability,' with 19,670 hectares burned and 9,361 people exposed in the burned area.

Re-checked 9 July 2026: GDACS's event report still shows a start date to last-detection window of 02 Jul 2026 to 08 Jul 2026 (duration 6 days), with 19,670 hectares burned and 9,361 people affected in the burned area unchanged; no thermal anomaly has been detected since 08 Jul 2026, indicating the fires are no longer actively spreading.

published.description: Multiple wildfires broke out across inland northern and central Portugal in early July 2026 amid a severe heatwave, drought-dry vegetation and strong winds, with the most serious fronts concentrated around the Viseu district. GDACS assessed an Orange alert for medium humanitarian impact as the fires spread. No further thermal anomalies have been detected since 8 July, indicating the fires are now contained.

impact.summary: GDACS's event report assessed an Orange alert (medium humanitarian impact) for wildfires burning across Portugal between 2 and 8 July 2026, citing about 19,670 hectares burned and roughly 9,361 people exposed within the fire-affected area. As of 9 July 2026, GDACS records no thermal anomaly detections since 8 July, indicating the fires are now contained.

location.region: Inland northern and central Portugal, centred near Viseu district

published.name: Wildfires — Portugal

claims.text: WHO Representative to Sudan Dr. Shible Sahbani reported more than 1,330 confirmed cholera cases and 114 confirmed deaths, a case fatality rate of 13.7 percent, in an outbreak affecting several states, mainly in the western part of the country, the Darfurs and the Kordofans, briefing journalists in Geneva on 10 July 2026. Aid officials say the true number of fatalities is likely much higher, and the disease is expected to spread further as the rainy season sets in, particularly among people who have fled towns and rural areas in North Kordofan.

UN News report (14 July 2026), citing OCHA, reconfirms WHO's count of 1,330 confirmed cholera cases and 114 deaths as of 7 July 2026, and describes the outbreak's continued spread: new confirmed and suspected cases in Kalma/Kampala displacement camp (South Darfur) and Tawila (North Darfur); West Kordofan reporting the highest caseload with new suspected cases and deaths; and North Kordofan reporting 32 new suspected cases in Gharb Bara locality in a single day. WHO delivered 8.5 tonnes of medical supplies to Kadugli and Dilling, South Kordofan — its first shipment there since December 2024 — and UNICEF delivered health supplies to El Fula, West Kordofan, serving about 429,000 people.

published.description: Sudan's third cholera wave in as many years has spread across war-affected western and central states, principally Darfur and Kordofan, where conflict and constrained humanitarian access are hampering the response. WHO warns the outbreak could worsen further as the rainy season sets in and displacement continues around besieged cities such as El Obeid. Mid-July reporting shows continued geographic spread into new localities in North and West Kordofan and South Darfur.

impact.summary: WHO's Representative to Sudan reported (10 July 2026, Geneva briefing) more than 1,330 confirmed cholera cases and 114 confirmed deaths, a case fatality rate of 13.7%, in an outbreak affecting several western and central Sudanese states, mainly Darfur and Kordofan. That aggregate remained WHO's latest confirmed count as of 14 July 2026, but OCHA reporting from mid-July describes active geographic spread — new confirmed and suspected cases in Kalma/Kampala displacement camp (South Darfur), Tawila (North Darfur), West Kordofan and North Kordofan — as WHO and UNICEF scale up medical supply deliveries to Kadugli, Dilling and El Fula. Aid officials say the true toll is likely higher, and WHO warns the outbreak could worsen as the rainy season continues and conflict constrains the humanitarian response.

location.region: Darfur and Kordofan states, western and central Sudan

published.name: Cholera Outbreak — Sudan

claims.text: UNHCR's Operational Data Portal for the Sudan situation records Total Forcibly Displaced at 11,431,220 (last updated 06 July 2026), comprising 6,597,438 IDPs inside Sudan (last updated 15 June 2026), 273,081 self-relocated refugees in Sudan (last updated 06 July 2026), and 4,560,701 total newly arrived refugees, asylum-seekers and returnees outside Sudan (last updated 06 July 2026).
UNHCR describes the Sudan situation as the world's largest displacement, humanitarian and protection crisis.

ReliefWeb's 'Population Movements from Sudan' report (06 July 2026) corroborates UNHCR's Operational Data Portal total of 11,431,220 forcibly displaced as the most current published figure; no later aggregate total-displaced update had been published as of 17 July 2026.

published.description: The conflict that erupted in Sudan in April 2023 between the Sudanese Armed Forces and the Rapid Support Forces has produced what UNHCR calls the world's largest displacement crisis, forcing millions from their homes inside Sudan and into neighbouring countries.

impact.summary: As of UNHCR's Operational Data Portal update on 06 July 2026 (the most recent available as of 17 July 2026), Total Forcibly Displaced in the Sudan situation stood at 11,431,220 — 6,597,438 IDPs inside Sudan, 273,081 self-relocated refugees within Sudan, and 4,560,701 newly arrived refugees, asylum-seekers and returnees outside the country. UNHCR continues to describe Sudan as the world's largest displacement, humanitarian and protection crisis.

published.name: Sudan Displacement Crisis

claims.text: GDACS's report for Tropical Cyclone BAVI-26, covering 01 Jul 2026 00:00 UTC to 05 Jul 2026 06:00 UTC, assessed an overall Red alert with a maximum wind speed of 287 km/h (Category 5).
GDACS reported an exposed population of 1.1 million people in areas experiencing Category 1 or higher winds from Tropical Cyclone BAVI-26.

GDACS assigned Tropical Cyclone BAVI-26 a GDACS score of 2.5, citing potential for high humanitarian impact from wind, storm surge and rainfall given medium vulnerability in the Northern Mariana Islands.

GDACS's current CAP alert for the event (episode 26, issued 07 July 2026 04:00 UTC) records Tropical Cyclone BAVI-26 active from 01 to 07 July 2026 across Guam, the Northern Mariana Islands, Japan, Taiwan and China, with the overall event alert level remaining Red while the current episode is assessed at Orange (episode alert score 2), indicating the storm has moved on from the Guam/Mariana Islands area toward East Asia after making landfall near Rota.

GDACS's episode 30 report (issued 08 July 2026, 04:00 UTC) shows Tropical Cyclone BAVI-26's overall alert level remaining Red, with the current episode also assessed Red, as the storm tracks west-northwestward over the Philippine Sea toward the Philippine Area of Responsibility, Taiwan, Japan and China; cumulative population exposed to Category 1 (120 km/h) or higher winds across the event is now approximately 29.988 million (about 30 million), and maximum sustained winds over the event's history reached 287 km/h (Category 5).

GDACS's event report (episode 34, current as of 9 July 2026) shows the event window now spanning 01 Jul 2026 00:00 UTC to 09 Jul 2026 06:00 UTC, with the overall alert level remaining Red, maximum sustained wind of 287 km/h (Category 5), and cumulative exposed population of 38.5 million in Category 1 or higher winds across Guam, the Northern Mariana Islands, Japan, Taiwan and China.

GDACS's event report (current as of 10 July 2026) shows the event window now spanning 01 Jul 2026 00:00 UTC to 10 Jul 2026 00:00 UTC, with the overall alert level remaining Red, maximum sustained wind of 287 km/h (Category 5), and cumulative exposed population of approximately 52.978 million in Category 1 (120 km/h) or higher winds.

GDACS's event report (current as of 11 July 2026) shows the event window now spanning 01 Jul 2026 00:00 UTC to 11 Jul 2026 06:00 UTC, with the overall alert level remaining Red and cumulative exposed population of approximately 63 million in Category 1 or higher winds across Guam, the Northern Mariana Islands, Japan, Taiwan and China.

GDACS's event report (current as of 12 July 2026) shows the event window now spanning 01 Jul 2026 00:00 UTC to 11 Jul 2026 18:00 UTC, with the overall alert level remaining Red, maximum sustained wind of 287 km/h (Category 5), and a total exposed population of 35.3 million in Category 1 (120 km/h) or higher winds. GDACS's CAP alert for the current episode (episode 44, sent 11 July 2026 16:00 UTC, last modified 12 July 2026 08:35 UTC) records the alert urgency as 'Past'.

published.description: A tropical disturbance east-southeast of the Marianas organized into Tropical Depression 09W on 1 July 2026 and was named Bavi as a tropical storm on 2 July. Bavi underwent rapid intensification and reached Category 5 super typhoon strength by 4 July with sustained winds near 287 km/h, prompting GDACS to issue an overall Red alert as the storm tracked through Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands. After passing near Rota, the storm continued west, entering the Philippine Area of Responsibility on 8 July (locally named Inday), tracked across Taiwan and Japan's Ryukyu Islands, and made landfall in Zhejiang Province, eastern China, on 11 July before weakening over land.

impact.summary: Super Typhoon Bavi (locally named Inday in the Philippines) made landfall in Zhejiang Province, eastern China, at 23:20 local time on 11 July 2026, after peaking at Category 5 strength (287 km/h sustained winds) near Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands on 4-6 July. GDACS's event report (current as of 12 July 2026) keeps the overall alert at Red for an event window of 01 Jul 2026 00:00 UTC to 11 Jul 2026 18:00 UTC, with a population of 35.3 million exposed to Category 1 (120 km/h) or higher winds; the current alert's urgency status is now 'Past', consistent with the storm weakening after landfall. GDACS's report does not list a confirmed fatality count for the event; Philippine media reported at least 18-19 deaths from landslides and heavy rain on Mindanao, and China evacuated more than 1.7 million people in Zhejiang ahead of landfall, though neither figure comes from an allowlisted authoritative source.

location.country: Guam / Northern Mariana Islands (United States); made landfall in Zhejiang, China
location.region: Western North Pacific, having passed near Rota between Guam and Saipan, tracked through the Philippine Sea and made landfall near Taizhou, Zhejiang, China

published.name: Super Typhoon Bavi — Guam & Northern Mariana Islands

claims.text: On 25 June 2026 a magnitude 7.5 earthquake struck 16 km SW of Moron in northern Venezuela, west of Caracas.

OCHA's Situation Report #22 (15 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report a toll of 4,829 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the 24 June earthquakes; 17,907 people remain without housing.

OCHA's Situation Report #23 (16 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report a toll of 4,930 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the 24 June earthquakes, with more than 1,308 aftershocks recorded and 17,907 people remaining without housing. The additional appeal was raised to US$299 million, still targeting 1.3 million people over six months. EU Commissioner for Crisis Management Hadja Lahbib conducted a field visit to La Guaira together with the UN Humanitarian Coordinator to review response efforts.
The USGS is assessing earthquake-triggered landslide hazards generated by the 2026 Venezuela earthquake sequence.

As of 1 July 2026, nearly 2,000 deaths have been confirmed and more than 6,400 people rescued; 1.8 million people are in need of humanitarian assistance including 680,000 children across six affected states, with around 1,000 buildings and over 400 schools damaged and more than 600 aftershocks recorded since the initial quakes.

OCHA's Situation Report #14 (07 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report an official toll of 3,685 people dead and 16,740 injured, with 6,462 people rescued since the 24 June earthquakes. Authorities also reported that approximately 17,907 people lost their homes and that more than 1,076 aftershocks have been recorded, with at least seven (7) states impacted and La Guaira remaining the most affected state.

OCHA's Situation Report #15 (08 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report an official toll of 3,811 people dead and 16,740 injured, with 6,462 people rescued since the 24 June earthquakes; approximately 17,907 people lost their homes and more than 1,100 aftershocks have been recorded. An additional appeal for US$296 million was launched to scale up the earthquake response for 1.3 million people over the next six months.

A first assessment by the UN Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR) estimates direct physical damage to housing and infrastructure at some $37 billion, comprising $24 billion in damage to buildings and another $13 billion in infrastructure; at least 79 transitional camps have opened in stadiums and sports centres, hosting displaced residents and providing aid.

OCHA's Situation Report #18 (11 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that seventeen days after the earthquakes, authorities report a toll of 4,333 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the onset of the emergency; 1,171 aftershocks have been recorded, 17,907 people remain without housing, and 315 of those who died have yet to be identified. UN humanitarian chief Tom Fletcher concluded his four-day visit to the country and called for sustained international support for the Humanitarian Response Plan; an additional appeal for US$298 million was launched to address the multisectoral needs of 1.3 million people over the next six months.

OCHA's Situation Report No. 20 (13 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that, nineteen days since the earthquakes, authorities report a toll of 4,561 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the onset of the emergency, with more than 1,254 aftershocks recorded and 17,907 people remaining without housing. Authorities report at least 856 affected buildings, including 190 that have collapsed. The response continues with 107 transitional camps supporting affected populations, with a capacity of 25,064 people and a current occupancy of 20,231 people. A preliminary UNDP estimate indicates direct physical damage of US$6.7 billion, equivalent to 6 percent of GDP, alongside the earlier UNDRR estimate of approximately US$37 billion. The Government of Venezuela announced the creation of an initial reconstruction fund equivalent to US$200 million.

OCHA's Situation Report #21 (14 July 2026, 9:00 pm) states that authorities report a toll of 4,734 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the 24 June earthquakes; 17,907 people remain without housing.

published.description: A magnitude 7.5 earthquake struck northern Venezuela west of Caracas on 25 June 2026, 39 seconds after a magnitude 7.2 foreshock in the same sequence. The USGS assessed that high casualties and widespread damage are probable, and the national government of Venezuela declared a state of emergency. More than three weeks on, the confirmed death toll has continued to climb past 4,900, and the UN has assessed billions of dollars in physical damage.

impact.summary: As of OCHA's Situation Report #23 (16 July 2026, 9:00 pm, published 17 July), authorities report an official toll of 4,930 people dead and 16,740 injured, with 6,462 people rescued since the 24 June earthquakes; 17,907 people remain without housing and more than 1,308 aftershocks have been recorded. An additional appeal, now raised to US$299 million, targets 1.3 million people over six months, on top of the US$632 million 2026 Humanitarian Response Plan covering 5.5 million people. EU Crisis Management Commissioner Hadja Lahbib conducted a field visit to La Guaira with the UN Humanitarian Coordinator to review the response. Earlier assessments put direct physical damage at $37 billion (UNDRR) and $6.7 billion / 6% of GDP (UNDP).

location.region: Near Morón / San Felipe, northern Venezuela (west of Caracas)

published.name: Venezuela M7.5 Earthquake Doublet
